one_line_takeaway: Agent skills are a real supply-chain prompt-injection surface: hiding the payload in a helper script and auto-rewriting SKILL.md to front-load that script as a 'required first step,' then refining via trace feedback, lifts payload-execution ASR from ~0% (naive) and 32% (prior manual) to ~81% on Claude Code, evading current prompt defenses and skill scanners.
key_finding: Automating inducement generation plus payload hiding dramatically outperforms naive and prior manual injection. On Claude Code the Naive baseline scores 0.0% ASR across all four categories and all four backends, while SKILLJECT averages 80.7% overall (GLM-4.7 97.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 94.7%, GPT-5-mini 83.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 47.0%). The attack generalizes to OpenClaw (80.5% overall) and transfers across models/tasks. Against the manual Skill-Inject baseline under identical benign-skill and payload conditions, average ASR rises from 32.3% to 69.1%. Stronger frontier models resist better (GPT-5.4 28.7%, Claude-Opus-4.6 29.2%) but are not immune, and both evaluated defenses only partially help: a runtime instruction-level defense cuts average ASR from 97.3% to 48.3% on GLM-4.7 (still 66% PrivEsc, 77% UnauWri), and static scanners average only 61.5% detection accuracy. The ablation shows front-loaded inducement (97.2%->78.8% when removed) and refinement feedback (->80.5%) matter most, payload understanding least (->91.0%).
core_contribution: SKILLJECT is presented as the first automated framework for generating and refining poisoned 'agent skills' (the SKILL.md-centered capability packages standardized by Anthropic's Agent Skills spec and supported by Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, etc.). The paper's own contribution is a two-channel skill-poisoning design plus a closed-loop multi-agent refinement pipeline: (1) an artifact channel that hides the malicious payload inside an auxiliary helper script (.sh/.py) appended to the skill's artifact set, and (2) an instruction channel that rewrites SKILL.md with a 'front-loaded inducement' strategy, placing an injected instruction at the very top of the document, framing the helper script as a mandatory prerequisite / required first step, and referencing its exact path plus an executable example command. The pipeline couples three agents: an Attack Agent that generates/rewrites the injected SKILL.md, a Victim Agent that runs downstream tasks with the poisoned skill, and an Evaluate Agent that inspects execution traces to decide whether the hidden helper script executed and returns diagnostic feedback (ignored/refused/partially-triggered/incorrectly-executed). The Attack Agent iterates on the documentation while keeping the hidden payload fixed, isolating inducement quality from payload capability. This is distinct from the cited prior work (Skill-Inject, SkillAttack, Schmotz et al.), which the paper characterizes as manual, per-skill, non-refined injection.
threat_model: Black-box supply-chain attacker who can publish or distribute a skill package through channels the victim agent can access (public skill registries such as ClawHub, shared repos, importable bundles). The attacker can arbitrarily modify both SKILL.md and bundled artifacts before distribution, but after installation cannot intervene in the victim agent's execution and does NOT control the victim's router/retrieval, model parameters, system prompts, or safety mechanisms. Crucially it is a post-selection threat model: the attack only takes effect once the (relevant) poisoned skill is actually selected and executed for a downstream task; the paper explicitly does not attempt to force the router to pick a malicious skill for unrelated tasks. Attack success is defined at the payload-execution (trace) level M(tau;B)=1 iff the hidden helper script is explicitly executed in the trace, not full realization of downstream harm (which depends on sandbox/permissions/network/user-approval). Four target behavior categories B: Information Disclosure (exfiltrate env vars like AWS_ACCESS_KEY or local files), Privilege Escalation (alter system config/file permissions for elevated access), Unauthorized Write (modify/delete/encrypt files outside the authorized workspace), and Backdoor Injection (execute a script or present a malicious URL disguised as legitimate).
limitations: Author-stated: (1) post-selection assumption — the poisoned skill must first be selected by the agent's router/retrieval; forcing selection for unrelated tasks is out of scope; (2) benchmark is 100 skills from a single platform (ClawHub) and may not cover all ecosystems/scaffolds/deployment policies; (3) agent behavior can vary with different system prompts, tool permissions, sandbox configs, and human-in-the-loop approval, which are not exhaustively swept; (4) defense evaluation only covers a prompt-level runtime mitigation and existing scanners. Reviewer-added: ASR is measured only at helper-script-execution level, not realized end-to-end harm, so numbers upper-bound real-world impact; the Attack Agent is an older/weaker model (GPT-3.5-Turbo) yet still succeeds, but no sensitivity analysis on Attack-Agent choice is given; success on the most safety-aligned victims (Claude-Sonnet-4.6 47%, Opus/GPT-5.4 ~29%) is markedly lower, and the very high numbers concentrate on GLM/MiniMax/DeepSeek backends; the refinement budget (up to 5 iterations) means reported ASRs include multiple attempts per skill.

datasets_used: Custom benchmark of 100 real community-contributed skills scraped from ClawHub, each paired with a hand-built task + workspace files, spanning four domains (Software Dev & DevOps, Data & Financial Analysis, Content Creation & Marketing, Research & Agent Tooling)
30-skill random subset of the 100-skill benchmark used for the newer-frontier-model evaluation
Preliminary measurement set of malicious skills collected from public online sources (Fig. 2 motivation)
Skill-Inject's own clean skills and malicious scripts, reused for the head-to-head comparison
quantitative_results: Claude Code overall ASR: Naive 0.0% vs SKILLJECT 80.7% average (GLM-4.7 97.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 94.7%, GPT-5-mini 83.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 47.0%)
Per-category average ASR (Claude Code): InfoDisc 78.8%, PrivEsc 81.0%, UnauWri 83.5%, Backdoor 79.5%
OpenClaw overall ASR: Naive 0% vs SKILLJECT 80.5% (GLM-4.7 97%, MiniMax-M2.1 95%, GPT-5-mini 87%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 43%)

Newer frontier models (30-skill subset) overall SKILLJECT ASR 68.4%: DeepSeek-V4-flash 95.8%, GLM-5.1 92.4%, MiniMax-M2.7 90.2%, MiMo-V2.7 74.3%, GPT-5.4 28.7%, Claude-Opus-4.6 29.2% (Naive 0.0% on all)
vs Skill-Inject (same skills/payloads): overall 32.3% -> 69.1%; GLM-4.7 40.0->82.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 35.6->80.9%, GPT-5-mini 26.7->64.4%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 26.7->48.9%
Cross-model transfer (generated on GLM-4.7, no target refinement): MiniMax-M2.1 79.8%, GPT-5-mini 49.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 35.8% (drops of 17.4/47.4/61.4 pts vs GLM-4.7 source 97.2%)
Cross-task transfer on GLM-4.7: original-task ASR ~95-98% (Backdoor 98, InfoDisc 98, PrivEsc 95, UnauWri 98) vs cross-task 70-80% (80/72/77/70)
Feedback refinement over 5 iterations: GLM-4.7 97.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 94.6%, GPT-5-mini 83.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 52.8%
Ablation on GLM-4.7: full 97.2% overall; w/o payload understanding 91.0%; w/o front-loaded inducement 78.8%; w/o refinement feedback 80.5%
Instruction-level prompt defense on GLM-4.7: average ASR 97.3% -> 48.3% (Backdoor 98->40%, InfoDisc 98->10%, PrivEsc 95->66%, UnauWri 98->77%); 49.0 pt absolute reduction
Static skill-scanner detection accuracy on SKILLJECT poisoned skills (GLM-4.7): overall 61.5% (ClawGuard Auditor 65.0%, SlowMist 64.2%, Cisco Skill Scanner 63.8%, Skill Vetter 53.1%)
SNS (structural naturalness, GLM-4.7) Naive->SKILLJECT: Backdoor 0.223->0.429, InfoDisc 0.318->0.415, PrivEsc 0.340->0.429, UnauWri 0.259->0.462
Motivating measurement (Fig. 2): publicly collected malicious skills achieve only 2.5-7.5% attack success across four agents, with the rest Rejected or Ignored
baselines_compared: Direct Injection (Naive) - explicit malicious commands written straight into SKILL.md; 0.0% ASR everywhere
Skill-Inject [31] - prior manually constructed malicious-skill attack; 32.3% average ASR
Prior Manual Attack (paradigm-level comparison, Table IV) - per-skill manual crafting, no batch, no refinement; 32.3% overall ASR
Instruction-level runtime prompt defense (script-inspection security directive) - defense baseline
Four static skill scanners: Cisco Skill Scanner, Skill Vetter, SlowMist Agent Security, ClawGuard Auditor - defense baselines
